text: The camera shows the interior of the abdominal cavity, highlighting the cyst to be removed. The uterus, bowels, and surrounding bowel fat are visible. The surgeon examines the origin of the cyst within the abdominal structures.
The fallopian tube is visibly stretched over the cyst, which is typically associated with ovarian origin. The surgeon examines the sides, revealing the ovary peeping from beneath the cyst.

The surgeon identifies a para-ovarian or paratubal cyst, noting that the ovary is completely separate from the cyst. The cyst arises from tissues below the fallopian tube, visibly stretching the fallopian tube.

The surgeon opens the capsule of the cyst and begins enucleation, carefully peeling the cyst away from surrounding tissues. The cyst walls are visibly vascular, requiring the use of an energy source to control bleeding at the margins.

The surgeon carefully peels the cyst out of its capsule, using a gentle technique to avoid damaging surrounding tissues. The cyst is nearly fully extracted, with only a small portion remaining attached.
The surgeon has nearly completed the enucleation of the para ovarian cyst, with the cyst visibly separated from surrounding tissues and almost fully removed from the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully addresses the bottom part of the para-ovarian cyst, where the blood supply is concentrated, using blood vessel sealers to prevent bleeding during dissection.

The cyst is now completely separated from surrounding tissues. The surgeon spreads the fallopian tube to check for any damage, confirming there is none. A large hole is visible at the site where the cyst was previously located. The surgeon prepares to open the bottom of the hole to prevent potential bowel obstruction.

The surgeon inspects the left fallopian tube and ovary, observing a blue dye being pushed through the tube. The dye fills the left fallopian tube, creating a visible blue tinge, but does not spill out. On the right side, the fallopian tube does not fill with dye, indicating a lack of dye entry.

The surgeon irrigates the surgical area with saline water to remove residual tissue and blood particles, enhancing healing and reducing complications. A custom-made bag is inserted to safely extract the cyst without rupture.

The surgeon pushes the cyst into a custom-made bag within the abdominal cavity. The bag is shaken to facilitate the movement of the cyst towards the opening. The mouth of the bag is then extracted through a one-centimeter incision. The contents of the cyst are suctioned out, causing the cyst to collapse.

The surgeon spreads the mouth of the bag to reveal the cyst, which is then carefully pulled out from the abdomen. The cyst is fully extracted, and the bag is removed, leaving the abdominal cavity clean.
